pageHero.heading: Our Services
pageHero.intro: At Elevate Mental Health and Wellness, we provide compassionate, evidence-based psychiatric care for children, adolescents, adults, and families. We understand that every mental health journey is unique. That is why we take the time to deeply listen, understand your concerns, and develop a personalized treatment plan aligned with your specific goals.

services.intro: Explore the mental health conditions we support. Tap any card to learn more about symptoms, causes, treatment options, and resources.

items.title: ADHD
items.summary: Support for focus, impulsivity, and daily functioning with treatment plans designed for school, work, and home life.

overview.heading: What is it?
items: ADHD affects how the brain works; it can make it hard to focus and stop yourself from acting on urges.
It can make it tough to pay attention and stay organized.
It can also affect how you feel and how you get along with others.
ADHD is not caused by a person's choices or behavior; it's a condition that starts in childhood.
ADHD is common; many adults have it, so you're not alone.

causes.heading: What can cause it?
items: Having someone in your family with ADHD
Differences in how the brain looks or works
Exposure to smoking, alcohol, or drugs before birth
Being born too early (premature) or too small
Early exposure to lead
Growing up or living in a stressful home
Having other mental health conditions like anxiety or depression
causes.note: Many things can work together to cause ADHD. Having these things doesn't mean you'll get ADHD for sure.

signs.heading: What are common signs?
items: Getting distracted easily and forgetting things
Losing things or struggling to keep track of tasks
Feeling restless or having trouble sitting still
Interrupting others or making quick decisions
Starting things but not finishing them
Having trouble planning your day or meeting deadlines
Feeling frustrated or upset often

brain.heading: How does it affect the brain?
brain.description: In ADHD, these parts of the brain don't work well together. This can make it harder to focus, stay organized, control impulses, and manage emotions.

regions.name: Cerebellum
regions.description: Helps you move smoothly and manage time

regions.name: Basal ganglia
regions.description: Controls movement and helps you stick to routines

regions.name: Dorsolateral prefrontal cortex
regions.description: Helps you focus, plan, and control impulses

regions.name: Anterior cingulate cortex
regions.description: Helps you switch between tasks and manage emotions

regions.name: Striatum
regions.description: Helps you form routines and stay motivated

seekHelp.heading: When should I seek help?
routine: If ADHD is making it hard to keep a job, stay safe, or manage your mood
If staying organized or focused is consistently difficult
If work or relationships are being affected because of ADHD symptoms
seekHelp.urgentHeading: Get help right away if you:
urgent: If impulsive decisions are causing ongoing problems

treatment.heading: How can it be treated?
treatment.description: Your health professional can help you choose the best treatment or combinations of treatments for you. Sometimes, people need to try a few different things before they find what works best.

resources.heading: Where can I get more help?

keyFacts.heading: What you need to know
items: ADHD is a medical condition caused by differences in brain function, not laziness or lack of willpower.
Many adults were not diagnosed as children, but symptoms continue into adulthood.
Medicine, therapy, and healthy habits can help you focus and feel more in control.
Many adults have ADHD, and support is available to help manage it.

items.description: Support for focus, impulsivity, and daily functioning with treatment plans designed for school, work, and home life.

items.title: Bipolar Disorders
items.summary: Stabilizing mood shifts with close monitoring, clear communication, and a plan that fits your routine.

overview.heading: What is it?
items: A brain condition that causes severe mood changes
People with bipolar disorder have very high ups (mania) and very low downs (depression)
These mood changes can last for days or weeks
Bipolar disorder can make it hard to work, take care of yourself, or do daily tasks
Health professionals can treat bipolar disorder with medicine and talk therapy

causes.heading: What can cause it?
items: It can run in families (passed down from parents)
Changes in the brain play a role
Stressful or hard life events can trigger it
It may start after a big life change
Most people get it between ages 6 and 30
Other mental health problems may increase the chance of bipolar disorder
causes.note: Health professionals are still learning about what causes bipolar disorder. It's usually not just one thing, but a mix of different reasons.

signs.heading: What are common signs?
items: Feeling super happy and full of energy (or very grouchy) for days (mania)
Feeling very sad and tired for long periods (depression)
Not needing much sleep or sleeping too much
Thinking and talking very fast
Doing risky things without thinking first
Having severe mood swings that last for days or weeks

brain.heading: How does it affect the brain?
brain.description: In bipolar disorder, some of these areas might work differently than usual. This can affect how a person feels, thinks, or acts.

image.alt: Brain regions affected by bipolar disorder

regions.name: Prefrontal cortex
regions.description: Helps control your actions and emotions

regions.name: Thalamus
regions.description: Passes messages in the brain

regions.name: Amygdala
regions.description: Handles fear and worry

regions.name: Hippocampus
regions.description: Helps with memory

regions.name: Striatum
regions.description: Helps you move, feel good, and stay motivated

regions.name: Hypothalamus
regions.description: Controls sleep, stress, and other body functions

seekHelp.heading: When should I seek help?
routine: You have very high energy and can't sleep for days
You feel very sad and don't want to do anything
You have severe mood swings that make life hard
You do risky things you wouldn't usually do
You feel worried, angry, or scared for no reason
You have trouble at work, school, or with family
You feel like hurting yourself
You hear or see things that aren't real
seekHelp.urgentHeading: Get help right away if you:
urgent: You feel out of control or very confused

treatment.heading: How can it be treated?
treatment.description: Your treatment may change depending on your mood: high (mania), low (depression), or steady (stable). Health professionals use these treatments together to help people with bipolar disorder feel better and live well.

resources.heading: Where can I get more help?

keyFacts.heading: What you need to know
items: Bipolar disorder is a brain condition that causes severe mood changes. These changes can make daily life hard.
It is a lifelong condition, but medicine, therapy, and healthy habits can help you manage it.
You are not alone. Many people have bipolar disorder. Health professionals, family, and friends can help.

items.description: Stabilizing mood shifts with close monitoring, clear communication, and a plan that fits your routine.

items.title: Depression
items.summary: Treatment for low mood, low energy, and loss of interest with a focus on helping you feel like yourself again.

overview.heading: What is it?
items: A common mental health problem
Feeling very sad for a long time and not enjoying things you used to like
Lasts for at least 2 weeks and makes it hard to do everyday activities
It's more than just feeling down for a short time. It's a serious condition that affects how a person lives their life

causes.heading: What can cause it?
items: Sad or stressful things happening in your life
Having family members who've had depression
Being someone who often feels sad or worries a lot
Having a big health problem that lasts a long time
Using drugs or drinking too much alcohol
Not having friends or family to help you
causes.note: Depression usually happens because of a mix of these things, not just one. Everyone's experience is different.

signs.heading: What are common signs?
items: Feeling very sad most of the time
Not liking things you used to enjoy
Eating or sleeping more or less than usual
Feeling very tired all the time
Finding it hard to think clearly
Feeling like you're not important
Thinking about dying

brain.heading: How does it affect the brain?
brain.description: In depression, some of these areas might work differently or look smaller than usual. This can affect how a person feels, thinks, or acts.

regions.name: Prefrontal cortex
regions.description: Helps control emotion

regions.name: Thalamus
regions.description: Passes messages in the brain

regions.name: Amygdala
regions.description: Handles fear and worry

regions.name: Hippocampus
regions.description: Helps with memory

regions.name: Striatum
regions.description: Helps you move, feel good, and stay motivated

seekHelp.heading: When should I seek help?
routine: You feel very sad for more than 2 weeks
You have trouble doing everyday things
You don't feel better on your own
You think about hurting yourself
You feel like life isn't worth living
seekHelp.urgentHeading: Get help right away if you:
urgent: Stay away from friends and family
Use drugs or alcohol to feel better

treatment.heading: How can it be treated?
treatment.description: Your health professional can help you choose the best treatment or combinations of treatments for you. Sometimes, people need to try a few different things before they find what works best.

resources.heading: Where can I get more help?

keyFacts.heading: What you need to know
items: Depression is a serious health problem
Depression is not your fault
Anyone can get depression
Depression doesn't just go away on its own
Health professionals can help treat depression

items.description: Treatment for low mood, low energy, and loss of interest with a focus on helping you feel like yourself again.

items.title: PTSD
items.summary: Trauma-informed care for flashbacks, hypervigilance, and emotional distress after difficult life experiences.

overview.heading: What is it?
items: PTSD means past trauma still affects you. It can happen after scary or dangerous events, like accidents, war, or abuse.
It can make you feel upset or afraid even when you're safe. Loud noises, certain places, or memories can bring back bad feelings.
You may have trouble sleeping or feel on edge. Nightmares, bad dreams, or feeling jumpy are common.
PTSD can cause strong emotions. You might feel sad, angry, or alone, and sometimes it's hard to enjoy things you used to like.

causes.heading: What can cause it?
items: Going through or seeing something very scary or dangerous (trauma)
Seeing someone else get hurt or killed (trauma)
Having a history of trauma or stress
Not having support after a trauma
Having anxiety, depression, or other mental health issues
Using drugs or alcohol to cope
causes.note: Not everyone who goes through a trauma gets PTSD. Our brains and bodies deal with stress in different ways. Having support and good ways to cope can help protect you from PTSD.

signs.heading: What are common signs?
items: You may keep thinking about the scary event or have bad dreams
Feeling scared or nervous all the time
Avoiding people or places that remind you of the event
Feeling angry, sad, or numb
Trouble sleeping or focusing
You may blame yourself for what happened, even if it wasn't your fault

brain.heading: How does it affect the brain?
brain.description: In PTSD, some of these areas might work differently or look smaller than usual. This can affect how a person feels, thinks, or acts.

regions.name: Prefrontal cortex
regions.description: Helps you make choices and control your feelings

regions.name: Thalamus
regions.description: Sorts information from your senses

regions.name: Amygdala
regions.description: Spots danger and creates feelings

regions.name: Hippocampus
regions.description: Stores memories and links them to feelings

regions.name: Brainstem
regions.description: Manages breathing, heartbeat, and sleep

seekHelp.heading: When should I seek help?
routine: You have nightmares or scary thoughts about a past trauma
You feel more angry, scared, or sad than usual
You are avoiding things that remind you of a past trauma
You have trouble sleeping or focusing
You feel alone or don't want to be with others
Have thoughts of hurting yourself or others
seekHelp.urgentHeading: Get help right away if you:
urgent: Feel so upset you can't do daily tasks
Use drugs or alcohol to cope with your feelings

treatment.heading: How can it be treated?
treatment.description: Your health professional can help you choose the best treatment or combinations of treatments. Sometimes, people need to try different treatments before they find what works best.

resources.heading: Where can I get more help?

keyFacts.heading: What you need to know
items: PTSD can happen after very scary events or trauma
It causes nightmares and scary feelings
You might avoid things that remind you of what happened
It's not your fault
Health professionals can help you feel better

items.description: Trauma-informed care for flashbacks, hypervigilance, and emotional distress after difficult life experiences.

items.title: Schizophrenia
items.summary: Careful evaluation and ongoing support for symptoms that affect perception, thinking, and daily stability.

overview.heading: What is it?
items: A serious brain problem that changes how people think and act
People with schizophrenia might see or hear things that aren't really there
They might think things are true even when others tell them it's not real
It can make it hard for people to do everyday things like work or take care of themselves

causes.heading: What can cause it?
items: It often runs in families, so you might get it if your parents or siblings have it
Problems during pregnancy or birth, like being born too early or at a low weight
Very stressful life events, like losing a loved one or being abused as a child
Using drugs like marijuana, especially when you're young
Living in a city
Having a hard time in school or with making friends
causes.note: Remember, having these things doesn't mean you'll definitely get schizophrenia. Health professionals are still learning about what causes it.

signs.heading: What are common signs?
items: Seeing or hearing things that aren't really there, like voices or people
Believing things that aren't true, even when others say they're not real
Having trouble thinking clearly or speaking in a way that makes sense
Feeling less interested in doing things and showing less emotion
Moving in unusual ways, like being very still or moving a lot for no reason

brain.heading: How does it affect the brain?
brain.description: In schizophrenia, some of these areas might work or look smaller than usual. This can affect how a person feels, thinks, or acts.

regions.name: Prefrontal cortex
regions.description: Controls how we think and behave

regions.name: Basal ganglia
regions.description: Helps control how we move and think

regions.name: Thalamus
regions.description: Passes information in the brain

regions.name: Amygdala
regions.description: Handles emotion and fear

regions.name: Hippocampus
regions.description: Controls memory and learning

seekHelp.heading: When should I seek help?
routine: You think about hurting yourself or others
You act very strange all of a sudden
You can't eat or clean yourself
You feel strange for more than 2 weeks
You have trouble doing normal things
You see or hear things others don't
You believe things that aren't true
seekHelp.urgentHeading: Get help right away if you:
urgent: You stay away from friends and family
You can't think or talk clearly

treatment.heading: How can it be treated?
treatment.description: Health professionals often use these treatments together to help people with schizophrenia feel better and live their lives.

resources.heading: Where can I get more help?

keyFacts.heading: What you need to know
items: Schizophrenia is a serious health problem
Schizophrenia usually starts when people are young adults
Health professionals can help treat it with medicine and other treatments
With treatment, many people with schizophrenia can live good lives

items.description: Careful evaluation and ongoing support for symptoms that affect perception, thinking, and daily stability.

footer.description: Individualized psychiatric care and brief psychotherapy for patients in California and Nevada.

paragraphs: Whether you are seeking support for anxiety, depression, ADHD, trauma, or behavioral concerns, our mission is to ensure you feel supported, informed, and empowered every step of the way.

items.title: Comprehensive Psychiatric Evaluation
paragraphs: A psychiatric evaluation is the essential first step toward understanding your mental health needs. During this thorough assessment, we gather information about your current symptoms, personal and family history, medical background, and life experiences.

To ensure an accurate diagnosis, we utilize evidence-based screening tools for a variety of conditions. This collaborative process allows us to build an individualized treatment plan tailored strictly to your unique strengths and goals.

items.title: Thoughtful Medication Management
paragraphs: Medication can be a highly effective component of mental health treatment. When medication is recommended, we work closely with you to review all available options, discuss potential benefits and side effects, and answer your questions without rush.

We provide ongoing, meticulous monitoring to ensure your medications remain safe and effective, and we make adjustments based on your response. When appropriate, we gladly coordinate care with your primary care provider, therapist, school team, or other specialists to ensure a seamless, comprehensive approach.

items.title: Integrated Psychotherapy and Psychoeducation
paragraphs: True mental health treatment goes beyond a prescription. We integrate supportive psychotherapy and psychoeducation into our appointments to help clients and families better understand their symptoms, develop healthy coping mechanisms, and navigate life challenges with confidence.
items.note: Note: While we do not provide intensive, long-term psychotherapy, these supportive elements are woven into our care to complement medication management and foster resilience.

items.title: GeneSight® Genetic Testing
paragraphs: As part of our commitment to personalized medicine, we offer optional GeneSight genetic testing to help guide clinical decisions. This simple test analyzes how your specific genes may influence your body response to certain psychiatric medications.
This testing may be highly beneficial if you have experienced:
bullets: Severe or unexpected medication side effects.
A history of multiple, unsuccessful medication trials.
items.note: Please note: Genetic testing does not diagnose mental health conditions, but serves as a valuable tool to minimize trial-and-error. Insurance coverage varies by plan, and out-of-pocket options are available.
